name: LDL receptor
summary: LDLR encodes the low-density lipoprotein receptor, a cell-surface glycoprotein that binds and internalizes LDL cholesterol particles. The receptor mediates endocytosis of cholesterol-carrying lipoproteins, regulating plasma cholesterol homeostasis. Mutations in LDLR cause familial hypercholesterolemia (FH), characterized by severely elevated LDL-C and premature coronary artery disease. GWAS have identified common variants near LDLR that modestly affect LDL-C levels and CAD risk. The gene is transcriptionally regulated by SREBP-2 in response to cellular cholesterol demand. Expression is highest in liver, adrenal cortex, and steroidogenic tissues.

regulatory_notes.promoter_activity: SREBP-2 binding elements drive cholesterol-responsive transcription; sterol regulatory element (SRE) in promoter.
regulatory_notes.enhancer_associations: CAD-associated variants map to regulatory regions modulating LDLR expression in hepatic and vascular cell types.
regulatory_notes.methylation_sensitivity: CpG methylation inversely correlates with LDLR expression in some tissues.

mechanistic_hypotheses.hypothesis: Reduced LDLR function elevates circulating LDL-C; oxidized LDL accumulates in arterial intima, triggering foam cell formation and atherosclerosis progression.
mechanistic_hypotheses.supporting_evidence: FH patients and LDLR-deficient mice develop accelerated atherosclerosis; statins upregulate LDLR and reduce CAD events.

references.title: GTEx Consortium. The GTEx Consortium atlas of genetic regulatory effects across human tissues
Genetic risk, adherence to a healthy lifestyle, and coronary disease
